A Look at Risk and Reward

If we compare the performance and risk figures of the two funds, we will actually see two very similar results.

In terms of volatility, measured by the standard deviation, there is no clear winner. In the short term, VDY is slightly less volatile, but it is not the case anymore in longer time frames. VDY’s standard deviation measures are 12.14% (3 years), 13.78% (5 years), and 13.35% (10 years) against 12.46% (3 years), 12.92% (5 years), and 12.48% (10 years). If we look beyond the daily volatility and try to see what is the worst thing that happened over the last 5 years with maximum drawdowns, we can make the same conclusion that there is no clear winner and the two figures are really close: -13.17% for VDY and -13.83% for VCE.

With a purely statistical lens, the two funds are equally risked. However, the important thing to keep in mind is that these metrics are backward looking and are not to be considered predictive. If over the last 10 years, the two funds behaved similarly, there is no guarantee that they will keep doing so in the next 10 years. In addition, if I consider the extreme concentration of VDY, I actually find the above results to be very surprising and would consider that you take a risk premium into account with your assessment of VDY. VDY’s positioning makes it very sensitive to macroeconomic events, both nationally and globally, as we are dealing with large-cap companies. For example, new regulations for the banking industry of variations or energy prices would have a very strong effect on VDY, while it would be mitigated (but still present) in the portfolio of VCE.

Let’s now look at the reward part with the total returns:

Again here, there is no clear winner. In the short term, VCE has provided investors with stronger returns, while the concentrated bet of VDY has proven to be a higher source of performance for investors. However, I don’t think that these returns are coming with the same level of risks. Let’s look at Sharpe ratios, measuring the additional performance produced by an additional measure of risk taken. For VDY, it is 1.13 (3 years), 1.20 (5 years), and 0.81 (10 years) and for VCE, it is 1.27 (3 years), 1.08 (5 years), and 0.83 (10 years). As you can see, the two ratios are very close, and there is not one that is the best in all time periods. However, this measure is based on the volatility, and fails to take other risks, such as concentration into account. If I do, it makes me think that VCE is the superior fund, and the one that will outperform consistently in the long term.

The Decisive Edge

In addition to the points we mention above, the financial metrics of the two funds are very different, further strengthening the case for VCE. The first one, and a compelling one is the expense ratio. As it is only tracking the market, VCE’s expense ratio is low at 0.06%. On the other hand, VDY is almost four times more expensive with an expense ratio of 0.22%. In absolute terms, this is not a very high difference, but given how close the two funds's returns have been, this is a difference that matters, especially since VDY is more expensive, it should justify it by a strategic advantage, that I have not seen until now.

Naturally, the focus of VDY on dividends results in a portfolio with a heavier “value” tilt, as the valuation metrics are indeed showing that. The fund’s P/E ratio is 15.0 and its P/B ratio is 1.9, in opposition with VCE’s metrics: 20.5 and 2.6. This means that VCE is more priced for growth, and given the similar performances so far, you could argue that this is in favor of VDY’s portfolio. However, I only think that this is a consequence of the sectoral split, and that you should not pay attention to it too much, given that the sectoral split is a weakness of VDY, not a strength. The valuation metrics are only consistent with it.

The core appeal of VDY is its distribution yield. The fund’s dividend yield is 3.53%, as opposed to VCE’s 2.53%. I actually find the difference to be really significant considering all the trade-offs made with VDY to get this higher dividend yield. You should ask yourself if the additional 1% in dividend yield that you get with VDY is worth the concentration risk taken, especially when total returns are so close. For me, it is not worth it.

I will finish on this part with a surprising fact: VDY is a lot more popular than VCE. The fund’s AUM is $4.8 Bn against only $2.7 Bn for VCE. The fund is also a bit more liquid with an average bid/ask spread of 0.03% against 0.05% for VCE. Of course, in both cases it remains comfortable enough, but I can’t find a convincing reason why VDY is so much more popular than VCE.

Risks

While if you only look at statistical risk measures you will think that the two funds are exposed to the same risks, it actually is not the case.

The first obvious one, that I already discussed a lot, is the concentration risk, especially for VDY. 80% of the portfolio invested in only two sectors is not a country specific ETF, it is a bet on the health of Canadian banks and the price of oil: not really what you would want to get as an income investor looking for low-risk investments. This exposes you to significant risks of sector specific shocks, and is further amplified by the single stock concentration of the fund. Of course, VCE is not perfect on this aspect and is also a concentrated fund, but compared to VDY, it is clear that the risk taken is much lower.

In addition, relying too much on two sectors introduces opportunity risks, filtering out sectors that are not paying high dividends. I have in mind the 0.74% allocation to tech, which is a real miss as it is a strong growth engine for the country with worldwide companies whose services are used everywhere like Shopify. The current market environment is still driven by tech, and getting no exposure to it as a country specific ETF is surprising.

In terms of sectors, VCE is also exposed to risks, but it is more about systematic market risk than it is about sector specific risk. And quite frankly, when investing in a country’s economy, there is no way that you avoid this risk. What you can do is make sure that your portfolio is well diversified so that you will suffer less from a broad market downturn, and while the figures of maximum drawdown are very close, I think that VCE is better equipped to navigate all kinds of shocks.

Conclusion

Both ETFs are strong options, and I rate them buys because they are so similar and, in their own way, provide investors with similar returns. However, I do think that VCE is a slightly better fund because it doesn’t carry the strong concentration risk of VDY (which can be an interesting feature for some), is significantly cheaper, and better representative of the Canadian Economy as a whole.

data.title: The Cooper Companies: Defensive Gem With Insider Buying And Building Momentum
data.description:
For months I have been focusing on defensive buy ideas. Another pick popping on my momentum screens this week is The Cooper Companies, Inc. (NASDAQ:COO). Cooper is a healthcare and medical products firm, manufacturing and selling contact lenses as its primary business (with the most contact wearers in the global marketplace at 43 million), plus major fertility and surgical divisions.

The company is growing at a steady pace (although at a slow rate). Cooper holds a rare tangible book value balance sheet in the medical products industry (around $14 per share), with very manageable net debt of $2.35 billion (vs. operating cash flow of $816 million over the past four quarters).

Four ideas stand out as the bull arguments in October 2025. First, looking at enterprise valuations of the operating business, Cooper is sitting at a decade-low position for new investment. Second, insiders have been heavy buyers of the stock since early September. Third, underlying trading momentum has actually been quite positive over the past several months. Fourth, COO has an investment history of outperforming the S&P 500 during recessions (I personally believe we are very close to a recession going into 2026).

Adding all four variables together, COO jumps out as a unique and credible purchase for long-term accounts. While many Big Tech stocks are trading at huge overvaluations, why not focus on finding value in the medical products sector, after a share price decline over a number of years?

If we do slip into recession and get a major turn lower in the U.S. equity market during 2026, I expect Cooper to be a solid outperformance winner vs. the S&P 500. Intelligent investors willing to grab an attractive set of value, growth, and defensive investment characteristics should be reviewing the Cooper upside story. I am initiating a Buy rating for Cooper, using a 12-month outlook for the stock.

The Business

A good overall review of the business is part of the latest August 2025 Investor Presentation here.

Revenue is running around $4 billion on a trailing basis, with close to $4 in non-GAAP cash EPS. Both are expected to rise slightly by the end of 2025. The positive operating business readout is that Cooper is a leader in contact lenses globally and runs a diversified list of fertility and surgical products as complementary units.

Positive Valuation Picture

The foundational driver for my investment interest is the low and sliding enterprise valuation setup. EV (which includes debt + equity) to core EBITDA (13.3x forward estimates) and sales (4.04x forward) have been trading at 10-year lows since summer. You have to go back to 2013 to find a lower valuation setup. Both numbers are now well below long-term averages back to 1990.

The forward 13.3x ratio of EV to EBITDA is considerably below the 20x multiple of three years ago, and sits in the middle of the pack vs. related peers/competitors in eye healthcare and medical products today.

The forward P/E estimate of 16.8x is well off the 31x number of July 2023, and trending toward the lower end of my peer sort group.

Free cash flow yield of almost 3% does not really grab your attention as a value play, but is in the middle of the peer group. I believe it could also be increased to 4% or even 5%, if the company slashed its relatively high (for the industry) capital expenditures.

Insiders Turning Bullish

Management has flipped from sellers of the stock in the $90s for price earlier in the year to buyers in the $60s during September.

The purchases started after Q3 FY2025 earnings were released, as the stock sold off on mildly disappointing GAAP EPS and lowered sales guidance for the second half of the year. My assumption is management is more optimistic about 2026, perhaps believing today's price and valuation are too low (representing something of a bargain vs. historical comparisons).

Attractive Chart Pattern (Bottoming)

Perhaps the primary reason for optimism over Cooper's immediate share price outlook is the momentum pattern since April. At that point, many indicators of stock trading health turned higher. I have circled in green below the bullish turns in the Accumulation/Distribution Line, Negative Volume Index, and On Balance Volume.

Despite the stock quote falling to new lows in August, a bullish divergence in these (and other) momentum signals could soon support a rising price trend. For sure, the large volume turnover since late May means shares have been exchanged between weak-hand holders (investors giving up on the price drop) to strong-hand owners (those looking for value and defensive characteristics over a longer period of time). I believe the background technical picture is actually quite strong to support price in the $60s.

Trading History During Recessions

The 2020 COVID pandemic, 2007-09 Great Financial Crisis, 2000-02 dot-com technology bust/recession, and 1990-91 Persian Gulf War economic contraction are the four episodes of sliding GDP growth since Cooper began public trading in 1986.

1990-91

The Persian Gulf War situation provided the first test of Cooper's defensive trading characteristics. Wall Street history tends to favor consumer staples and medical-related stocks during stress events in the economy. The thinking goes sales and demand will remain relatively stable for these firms, while consumers may cut back on travel, auto and home purchases, dining out, etc., if they are worried about losing their job (or actually become unemployed). You can review the meaningful outperformance gains achieved by Cooper during 1990 and early 1991 below.

2000-05

The next recession occurred on the original technology bust of 2000-02, which could be analogous to the AI-inspired Big Tech boom since 2023. Cooper performed quite well as the U.S. stock market peaked in 2000, while strong gains for stocks would follow for a stretch of years into late 2004.

2007-09

Between the S&P 500 peak of late 2007, through the worst months of the Great Recession, and following the recovery from economic turmoil into 2010, Cooper performed closer to in-line vs. the major U.S. market averages. The second half of 2010 did witness some extra gains by COO. So, long-term investors would have benefited from owning the stock during this period in history.

2020

Again, during the 2020 COVID pandemic shutdown, Cooper performed at a rate close to the S&P 500, although owning Big Tech would have been a more productive proposition coming out of the worst fears for the economy since 2009.

Overall, two recessionary periods proved a great time to own Cooper Companies, while two produced market-similar returns. The net average conclusion of all four is it would have been smarter to own COO during and slightly after the recession, than most other businesses.

Final Thoughts

Unfortunately, Cooper does not pay a dividend check quarterly. Instead, the company has decided to focus on share buybacks for the return of investor capital.

In this vein, management just upped its share repurchase program by another $1 billion (on a current equity market capitalization of $14 billion). With free cash flow in the $400 to $500 million range annually (going forward as my estimate), the company could easily retire 3% to 4% of outstanding counts each of the next two years, without taking on new debt.

When you combine this repurchase effort with the fact that Cooper is still spending a decent amount on CAPEX ($400+ million annually) for product improvements and bringing new ones to market, current Wall Street analyst estimates may prove on the low side of reality a few years out. In a recession, 10% growth rates for both "per share" income and sales would be a terrific result (in my view).

What could go wrong and lead to investment losses in Cooper? I would say a sharp market implosion (like a 1987-style crash on Wall Street or black-swan event like the 2020 pandemic) may prove too much for COO, even with its lower valuation profile.

In addition, if a deep recession hits in 2026, the valuation could move even lower, similar to how things played out during 2008-09. Yet, any oversized drop in COO should open up an even smarter area for long-term accumulation.

Cooper Companies represents yet another selection I have added to the defensive side of my portfolio. Since the summertime, I have talked about a variety of smaller medical product names and food makers as the areas I am concentrating my buying.

If we are on the verge of a 20% bear market or something worse in the 40% to 50% decline range from a serious recession, you will want to hold a long list of defensive ideas. Flight-to-safety money flows could easily reverse Cooper's downtrend in price during late 2025 with little warning.

Assuming history repeats, COO could soon morph into a solid outperformance selection (hedge to a degree) if we get a slow-grinding recession and bear market span like 2000-02 or 2007-09.

I rate Cooper Companies shares a Buy for a 12-month outlook. My forecast is COO will outperform the S&P 500 over the next year, even if small losses appear (measured against the potential for unusually large S&P 500 losses on one of the greatest market overvaluations ever on price to sales, total market cap to GDP, the 10-year CAPE Ratio).

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: U.S.-China Trade Tensions: From Complacency To Concern
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist

The latest

Over recent weeks, tensions between the United States and China have intensified, culminating in the U.S. threatening to impose an additional 100% tariff on Chinese imports starting November 1, alongside expanded controls on critical software exports.

Markets initially responded with a sharp risk-off shift, but a more measured tone from President Trump and China’s apparent reluctance to escalate further—despite intermittent flare-ups—helped restore a degree of calm. Nonetheless, this episode serves as a stark reminder of the latent risks in trade relations, an area that had faded from the macro spotlight, and has jolted market complacency.

Background

U.S. tariffs on Chinese goods had stabilized at approximately 54%, comprising a 30% increase atop pre-existing 24% levies. However, in late September, the U.S. announced a significant expansion of its export control regime, with major implications for Chinese firms’ access to U.S. technologies.

In response, China declared a substantial expansion of its own export controls, particularly targeting rare earth minerals—a critical input for AI development. Notably, China dominates this space, controlling over 90% of global supply. Given the concentration of tech in U.S. equity markets and the pivotal role of AI-related capex in supporting U.S. growth, this move poses a material threat to both the U.S. economy and markets.

Conversely, China remains heavily reliant on U.S. chipmaking equipment, and Beijing’s ongoing economic fragility suggests it too is ill-positioned for a prolonged trade war.

Forward-looking implications

Tariffs had largely receded from macro strategy discussions, owing to perceived stability and limited economic impact. However, the proposed 100% increased tariff would have significant potential impacts moving forward.

Tariff impact: A 100% levy would raise the effective China tariff to ~155%, lifting the U.S. average effective tariff rate from ~13% to nearly 30%—the highest in over a century, even surpassing post–Liberation Day levels.

Economic hit: Preliminary estimates suggest a ~3% drag on U.S. GDP and inflation breaching 4%, well above the Fed’s 2% target. Even if treated as transitory, the magnitude would likely compel a more cautious Fed stance.

Trade embargo risk: Such a steep tariff could trigger a de facto trade embargo. Following the April announcement of a 145% tariff, container traffic from China to the U.S. collapsed, raising fears of supply chain disruptions and empty shelves.

China’s vulnerability: While the U.S. would suffer, China’s export-dependent economy would also face significant strain, particularly amid domestic economic weakness.

Fiscal repercussions: A breakdown in trade could severely dent U.S. tariff revenues, potentially refocusing investor attention on fiscal deficits and exerting upward pressure on long-end Treasury yields.

Strategic outlook

The severity of a renewed trade war increases the probability of eventual compromise. However, a return to the previous status quo appears unlikely. President Trump may be recognizing China’s leverage in rare earths, potentially prompting a more conciliatory approach to re-engage negotiations.

The path to compromise is expected to be volatile. Further escalation remains possible, and even post-resolution, tariffs and non-tariff measures (such as export controls and investment restrictions) are poised to remain a key geopolitical lever, especially as the U.S. and China continue their strategic rivalry in AI and technology. Trade frictions are likely to persist as a feature of the global macro landscape.

Market outlook

Renewed tensions have exposed market fragility, particularly given stretched valuations and elevated sensitivity to growth shocks. Investor complacency around tariffs has been disrupted, prompting a reassessment of risk.

While a compromise is anticipated, markets may remain jittery in the near term. End-of-cycle concerns are mounting, and the government shutdown has created a data vacuum that amplifies uncertainty. Still, the broader macro backdrop remains constructive: resilient growth, underpinned by policy support (monetary and fiscal) and sustained transformative technology investment, suggests markets can withstand setbacks. Distinguishing between idiosyncratic and systemic shocks is crucial.

In this uncertain environment, a balanced, diversified approach remains essential—staying constructive on risk assets where fundamentals warrant, while managing valuation risks and maintaining portfolio resilience.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Middlesex Water: A Very Split Thesis, But With Upside
data.description:
In this article, I'll provide an update on the Middlesex Water Company (MSEX).

Middlesex is one of the water-based or water-oriented (business-wise) companies that I cover on Seeking Alpha. Other companies in the similar sector include York Water (YORW) as well as European companies either in water utilities, like Veolia (OTCPK:VEOEY), or industrial water infrastructure. My last article on Middlesex can be found here.

My previous article was over 4 months back, and my thesis at the time was a positive one. Because it's been almost half a year at this point, because the company has come up with new results, and because I believe the time has come to update the thesis (also a new PT for the company), I believe it's a good time for an update here.

When I last covered it, the valuation for the company had normalized from premium levels. While this company isn't exactly a "yield king" with a sub-4% dividend yield, water companies typically tend toward lower yields, and this is not an argument that makes the company uninvestable.

Instead, the business model, which includes regulated income similar to other utilities, is what creates the appeal for investing here. And in today's volatile world, there's definitely something to be said for investing in something safe and slow-growing but with the defensive nature that's not going anywhere - such as water, heat, electricity, food, or the like. Things that, regardless of societal change (which seems significant at this time), are going to remain mostly "as is".

Middlesex isn't the only company here, but it's one of the better ones.

Why?

Let me show you and use this article as a preview for the 3Q25 period, which is upcoming soon - also this update is relevant due to the recent (July) rate case decision, effective as of the 3rd of July this year.

Middlesex Water Company - Valuation Appeal And Upside Exist Here

Collecting it, treating it, distributing it, and also selling it, that's what Middlesex does with water. It's not just residential or commercial, but also industrial sales of water, and also specialized business areas, such as fire protection. The company's history includes growth from a single water utility to, over time, an enterprise with operating units. It's bigger than YORW, for instance.

While it does have commercial and industrial customers, the vast majority of its customer exposure remains residential. A big part of these companies is what sort of relationships they have with the regulator, because regulators are the deciding factor when it comes to how much these companies are "allowed" to earn. Pricing is typically set, and allowed to rise depending on maintenance and expansion needs, as well as an "allowed" profit or return, usually ROE. The company has been a dividend king for 2 years, meaning it's paid uninterrupted dividend increases for over 50 years. This is one of the few companies in the world, and in the US market, that has done this.

Furthermore, its fundamentals are cemented by an A rating from S&P Global, again not atypical for a water company; YORW also has this, but it's definitely good.

One of the things investors should pay attention to is the fact that the company doesn't just have regulated operations. This is one of the advantages that many of these utilities have started to include. The inclusion of non-regulated operations creates, theoretically, an economic buffer for when earnings from the regulated businesses aren't as good. Also, again theoretically, they offer a good use of excess cash for expansion and investing. Still, this company has a vast majority of regulated business operations.

The name of the game for MSEX, just as with many of these utilities, is infrastructure investment to get rate base growth. The current 6-year rate base growth averages 12.1% as of mid-2025 and the 2Q25 results, which is good.

It's definitely worth mentioning here that MSEX is expecting a heavier investment period upcoming. That means that EPS is going to be lower in terms of growth (usually flat, or 1-3% growth, as evidenced by recent forecasts for 2025E, which we'll go into in the valuation section), but it also obviously means infrastructure safety for the future. The last higher period of infrastructure investment was during the late 2010s and early 2020s, and the company's annual infrastructure investment is set to rise above $100M for the first time in over 5 years next fiscal, with a current estimate of $133M. For the next 3 years in total, almost $400M is expected, but this includes resilience and preventive investments as well, including a large $100M+ investment in a PFAS treatment plant, the company's RENEW program, replacement programs, and the like. In short, a lot of different things are set to make the company more attractive on a forward basis.

The recent base rate case decision from the regulator was a positive one. It allows an RoE of 9.5% at an equity ratio of 53.5%. The allowed RoE is determined with the regulator looking at things like capital structure, cost of debt, as well as other expenses. RoE represents the profit margin that is then compared to other enterprises with corresponding risk profiles, while at the same time allowing the company to survive (but not only that) and to attract capital. It's fair to say that the RoE in a rate case decision is key to what revenue we can expect, and then, going by the company's typical profit margin, we can see where it goes.

In New Jersey, the company has gone even higher in terms of what it wants. The rate case was filed on June 30th, effective in less than a year from now, where the company has requested an RoE of 10.5% - my expectation is between 9.5-10% here based on previous numbers. The company's requests represent a revenue increase request of almost $25M at a rate base of close to $650M.

During the second quarter, which is the last quarter we have to look at here, the company grew its revenues by less than 0.5%, but at least we saw growth. Net income and earnings were up by low single digits, below 2.5%, but again, positive for this sort of company.

However, I fully expect things to decline from here on out. The reason for this expectation is the company's extensive 3-year investment plan, which, as we're speaking and considering the company, is already ongoing. Expect, therefore, lower EPS for 2025. 2026 might be a year of growth, but it will largely depend on how exactly the rate case mentioned above turns out. Costs and pressures are heavy everywhere, and regulators are in a position where they have to both look at what is reasonable to expect customers to pay, while at the same time taking into consideration that the company needs to survive and be attractive to operate as it does. Not an enviable position to be in. Which side "wins out" depends on the situation, the case, and the geography.

The best thing MSEX can do to guarantee as good returns as possible is to have as little debt as possible (it's below 47.5% at this time), with maturities of less than $10M per year, and dropping to less than $8M in 2027E and forward. So on that basis, I'd say we're well-covered.

The above being said, I will mention that when it comes to MSEX and water utilities overall, water is still a cheap expense in the US (for the most part). For MSEX average customers, their water bill is less than 1% of their household income.

2Q25 was as expected - but I expect 3Q25 to be a bit more "telling" with regard to the increased investment requirements, meaning we might see a bit more of a downside here.

Valuation for Middlesex - The upside is still conservatively over 20% per year at this price.

In my last article, I made it clear that the company constitutes a SWAN investment, and that doesn't change here. The trading below 25x average P/E (slightly above the 24x P/E average of my last article), still puts the company both below peers, as well as, significantly below the average P/E for the shorter and medium term. The only argument to a significant downside here is if you were to argue that the company's 3-year investment plan represents a massive downside and an argument to value the company much, much lower than we've seen in the past.

I do not believe such an argument makes sense.

My last PT for the company was $62/share. That means buy below $62/share - and I believe the company has a fair value of close to $80/share, based on a forward estimate of around 28-29x P/E for the 2026-2027E period. In a bearish scenario, I would drop this to $75/share, provided that the rate case outcome in NJ is worse than expected. If the company gets a better result than expected (such as getting its requested RoE), I would raise this to $85/share. That's the range we're "moving in" here and the possibilities that I see. Based on the company's fundamentals, its typical earnings accuracy over time, and peer valuations, I consider this appealing enough for a continued "BUY". Because I forecast the 3Q25 period to potentially be below expectations, I believe a case can be made here to wait for those results prior to investing.

Peers are also appealing, is the thing. York Water comes to mind. The company has dropped to a 22.5x P/E, which is very low for this business. On a 5-year average, the upside for the company looks as follows until 2026E.

Even if we were to value the company at the same 29-30x P/E as MSEX, the upside is still over 40% on an annual basis inclusive of the dividend, with an implied FV of close to $50/share.

This means that while Middlesex is a "BUY", I believe YORW to be more attractive and the "BUY" I would consider at this time.

My risk considerations for the MSEX thesis are as follows.

Risks To My Thesis

Looking at the future, as mentioned, the one risk I see is that the combination of increased servicing and infrastructure costs, coupled with lower returns, would justify a fundamentally lower premium for water companies. This remains the risk going into 3Q25, and I believe the current forecast for the company's earnings confirms this. This risk is similar to YORW, but YORW is cheaper, which makes it, in turn, more attractive.

This is a risk I consider worth taking. If you're either, like me, fully invested in Veolia or similar companies or want a non-international stock, then what you have here is a good, safe upside and what I view to be a SWAN.

My thesis for the SWAN is as follows.

Thesis

Middlesex is a solid dividend king among other water companies, which share the company's fundamental qualities and upside. The positives for these companies include a safe dividend, safe operations usually unaffected by external turmoil due to the necessary qualities of their operations. Their risks are typically limited to local environmental disasters/effects, and the need for retrofitting or addressing their service infrastructure. Risks in this particular company are low, even if I view YORW as being a somewhat better and safer investment overall.

I give the company a PT of $62/share, unchanged from my previous article, going into 3Q25.

I therefore view Middlesex Water as a "BUY" and would consider opening a position in the company for the long term. I believe we have an annualized upside of at least 20% or higher here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (bolded).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a price target of $62/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of October 2025.

data.title: VGUS: Parking Spot For Cash
data.description:
Introduction

The Vanguard Ultra-Short Treasury ETF (NASDAQ:VGUS) is an ETF that allows you to park excess liquidity with a yield higher than that of a checking account or a traditional money fund. Its investment objective is to follow an index of US Treasuries, with maturities of less than 12 months, which means that the fund assumes a duration of 0.4 years and consequently a risk of almost non-existent duration. Its current expense ratio is 0.07% and is a fabulous vehicle to park credit on government debt, without looking for complications in corporate or mortgage tranches.

The index is monitored or replicated by the management team using the representative sampling technique. The Bloomberg Short Treasury Index remains a very clear and narrow index, as it has only Treasury bonds with maturities of less than 12 months. As a market-weighted index, the weight of each issue depends on the size of the outstanding balance; and because everything matures soon, the portfolio naturally rotates into new auctions and reissues.

All these features place the ETF at the shortest part of the interest rate curve. This implies that the VGUS investor will have a lower volatility investing here, than in funds with a longer duration. It will have yields that are fairly aligned with current official rates, owing to the relationship between interest rates and the price of money.

An important point in any analysis is to understand how an ETF makes money. The main source of return in this case is the interest earned on the treasury bonds held by the fund (carry), which is distributed as a monthly dividend. With everything less than 12 months away, the price risk from rate movements is small but not non-existent. In rate-increasing environments, NAV may fall somewhat (little, for a short duration), in downward environments, the price may rise slightly.

A relevant point that the reader may question is the difference between buying a money fund or a very short-term T-Bill ETF. Here every alternative has its place. In addition to T-Bills, MMFs often invest in repos and other very short-term debt instruments. While VGUS being an ETF is trading during the day and can be more comfortable to assign to a portfolio of ETFs. In contrast to a 0–3 month ETF (such as VBIL), VGUS extends the spectrum slightly up to 12 months, which smoothens rotation and at certain times can capture an additional premium if the 1–12 month slope is positive.

Analysis - Macro Fundamental

In this section, we will analyze some macroeconomic indicators in detail to try to offer the reader an accurate perspective and a sound recommendation on the vehicle in question.

The first relevant metric to analyze this ETF is the front-end carry (1-3M), compared to its slope and the interest rate on reserves balance (IORB). The main line (yield_nowcast) is the yield nowcast that the ETF can capture when it rotates its Treasury bills.

To calculate it, we take the average of the 1 and 3 months to approximate the “sweet spot” where the portfolio usually lives (all below 12 months, with the WAM very short). On that axis we add the slope of the 1-3M section, which indicates when the short section is slightly inverted and limits the additional carry. The second element is the difference between headroom (yield nowcast) and the IORB rate. In a situation where this headroom is positive, the bills offer a slightly higher return than the market “floor” (IORB rate).

Today, we can see that the nowcast of the fund has cooled, from the peak zone of the years 2023-2024 and has been stabilizing in an area near the IORB. On the other hand, the green line (headroom) is located in levels very close to zero. This situation is typical of market phases where haircuts are discounted or “extra” premium levels are required on the 1-3M tranche of bills. With this reading, we can draw several conclusions in this first metric:

  • The first is that today the fund continues to pass on the official rate pulse to its dividend, but with little “extra” margin above ground.
  • Secondly, the slope of tranche 1-3M with its flattening, suggests that the roll of that tranche is not adding hardly any carry, so that the profitability of the fund will follow very closely the evolution of the official rates.

In the second metric, the idea we have is to control the cash market. We do this by calculating a composite indicator consisting of three variables (the bank reserves deposited with the EDF, the levels of IRRs, and the AMT account). Each series is normalized with z-scores to compare different magnitudes on a common scale (higher z implies above its historical mean, lower z implies below). With these elements, the variable LIQ_z (which is equal to z(reserves) − z(RRP) − z(TGA) is assembled, and attempts to capture the “useful” level of liquidity for a money market, where more reserves tend to favor the circulation of cash, while a widely used RRP and a bulky TGA tend to absorb (the Treasury keeps more money in its account, and that money is not in the loop).

In addition, variations at 4 weeks of each component (Δ4w) are added to see the recent rate of change, because many times the turn of conditions comes by the weekly dynamics before it is seen at the absolute levels. With this panel, we get a “market fund” reading for the 0–12 month tranche where the ETF operates (a loose environment usually accompanies smooth auctions and orderly spreads; a more tense environment usually leads to longer queues and some friction in financing).

Reading the current graph, we show that the liquidity level (LIQ_z) moves close to zero, with certain impulses that do not consolidate over time. When the Δ4w of the RRP is negative and reserves do not show continuous drops, the bill market tends to operate normally, and the return on new issues aligns with the official rates, which for an ETF like VGUS translates into consistent monthly dividends and very contained price volatility. In those segments where the TGA accumulates tickets for several weeks, the circuit becomes more demanding (more competition for cash available, somewhat tighter auctions, less “space” above the rate floor).

Conclusion

As I see the current situation, I recommend keeping the analyzed vehicle in your wallet, if you already have it. As we have seen, the extra carry that could squeeze the ETF, is very limited at the moment. It is normal to assume a gradual decline in monthly distributions if the market consolidates expectations of cuts. In addition to this, we have the liquidity indicator that marks us a neutral point at the moment, which means that bills do not pay a large premium with respect to the market floor (IORB). Thank you for reading.
